title: Wet-felted mittens
subtitle: A seamless pair shaped over a flat resist template in solid cream or speckled grey merino
excerpt: Wet-felt a pair of seamless mittens for adult women's medium hands using a flat resist template cut to hand shape. Merino roving in solid cream or speckled grey is cross-hatched over both faces of the resist, felted to a firm fabric, then cut open at the wrist and the resist removed. The flat-resist mitten template is the standard wet-felting approach taught across Northern European felting traditions.
sourceNotes: Observation-led; the flat-resist mitten template is the standard wet-felting approach taught across Northern European felting traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat template placed between two layers of roving so that the wet-felting process bonds the fibres around the template's perimeter without creating a seam. When the resist is removed and the wrist edge cut open, the mitten is a single continuous piece of felt with no joins: the crown, thumb gusset, and palm are all one fabric.

content.text: These mittens are built over a
seamless resist

: a flat hand-shaped template cut from card or plastic, around which merino roving is layered on both faces and then felted into a single continuous shell. One mitten is felted at a time. The working resist is sized for adult women's medium hands with a shrinkage allowance built in. Merino in solid cream or speckled grey felts predictably and produces a fabric with enough density to block winter wind without being stiff. Finished wrist-to-fingertip length is approximately 24 cm; hand circumference around the knuckles approximately 20 cm. Allow two to three hours to complete both mittens.

attrs.title: Slip hazard
attrs.body: Work on a non-slip surface. Hot soapy water on a smooth floor is a slip hazard; mop spills as they happen.

content.text: What you need

items.name: Merino roving, solid cream or speckled grey
Hot water, around 50 to 60 degrees C
Cold water, for shocking
items.qty: A second bowl or tap access

items.name: Card or thin plastic sheet, for the resist template (see Step 1)
items.qty: 1 sheet per mitten, or cut two at once

items.name: Felting mat or bubble-wrap mat (bubbles up)
Palm fitter or smooth plastic bag, for rubbing
Sharp scissors, for cutting the wrist opening
A ballpoint pen, for tracing the hand outline

content.text: Method
Step 1: Cut the resist template

Place your hand flat on a sheet of card with the fingers together and the thumb extended to a natural resting angle. Trace around the hand with a ballpoint pen. Draw a cuff extending 6 cm down from the wrist. Now redraw the entire outline 2 cm outward from your traced line: this is the shrinkage buffer. The total template should be roughly 30 x 18 cm at this stage. Cut out the template cleanly; smooth any jagged edges. For the second mitten, trace the same hand with the thumb on the opposite side to get a mirror-image pair, or use the same template flipped over.
Step 2: Lay out the first face

Lay the felting mat flat. Place the resist on the mat. Pull thin, consistent wisps of merino from the roving and lay them in four layers over the resist face: two horizontal, two vertical, each at 90 degrees to the previous. Extend each wisp 3 cm beyond the resist edge all the way around. Pay extra attention to the thumb crotch (the angle where the thumb meets the palm): fibres must cross this area in both directions or it becomes a weak point in the finished mitten. Once the four layers are down, fold the extended edge fibres back over the resist edge onto the second face, pressing them flat.
Step 3: Lay out the second face

Flip the resist over carefully, keeping the first-face fibres in place. Lay four layers on this face in the same cross-hatch order: two horizontal, two vertical. Again fold the edge fibres back over onto the first face. Both faces now have four layers each and the edges are secured by the folded-back fibres running over the resist perimeter. Check that the thumb area has good coverage on both faces before covering with the felting net.
Step 4: Wet out and felt the first face

Mix hot soapy water in the bowl. Drizzle it slowly over the net-covered mitten, pressing down gently with a flat palm to saturate the roving without displacing the fibres. Once the whole face is saturated, rub through the net in firm, straight passes: 40 passes across the width of the mitten, then 40 passes along the length from wrist to fingertips. Give the thumb area extra attention: rub 10 passes along the thumb from base to tip. After 5 minutes of rubbing, lift the net and check: the surface should feel slightly bonded and no longer shift like loose fibres. If it feels very loose, re-cover and rub for a further 3 minutes.
Step 5: Flip and felt the second face

Slide a hand under the felting mat and flip the whole assembly over so the second face is up. Re-lay the net and repeat the wetting and rubbing process on this face: 5 minutes of firm rubbing in two orientations, with extra passes at the thumb. By the end of this stage, both faces should feel lightly cohesive but still spongy when pressed. Add fresh hot soapy water if the surface cools.
Step 6: Full to size

Transfer the mitten to the bamboo rolling mat. Roll the mat around the mitten and roll back and forth firmly for 3 minutes. Unroll, rotate the mitten 90 degrees, re-roll, and roll for a further 3 minutes. Check the dimensions by placing the mitten against the resist template: it should be shrinking and stiffening. Shock the mitten with cold water, dunking it in the cold bowl for 30 seconds, then return to hot soapy water and roll again. Repeat the hot-roll and cold-shock cycle three or four more times. The mitten is ready to open when the fabric is firm, dense, and no longer stretchy: squeezed, it should spring back rather than staying compressed. At this point the wrist-to-fingertip length should be around 24 cm and the circumference at the knuckles around 20 cm.
Step 7: Remove the resist and open the wrist

Locate the wrist edge of the mitten. The folded fibres at this edge form a closed seam. Use sharp scissors to cut straight across the wrist edge, 1.5 cm in from the fold: this creates the opening for the hand. Ease the resist template out through the wrist opening. The interior of the mitten should be smooth and the fabric should feel the same on both faces. If the resist sticks, the fabric is well felted; ease it out slowly by crumpling it slightly. Try the mitten on a hand while still damp: it should fit with a little resistance at the widest part of the knuckles. If it is too tight, stretch the damp felt gently over the hand while warm. If the wrist opening is too narrow, cut a further 1 cm from the edge. Repeat the entire process for the second mitten.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The thumb has felted shut and will not separate from the main mitten body
items.cause: Fibres from the palm face migrated across the thumb crotch gap and bonded with the thumb face during felting
items.fix: Snip the thumb seam open with scissors, ease a finger in, and rub the raw edges with soapy fingers to seal them. Next time, tuck a small strip of plastic into the thumb crotch before wetting. The plastic keeps the two faces apart during early felting.

items.problem: One mitten has come out noticeably smaller than the other
items.cause: The second mitten was rubbed and rolled harder or longer than the first, or the resist template was not used consistently for size checks
items.fix: Compare both mittens against the resist template at the end of the fulling stage before cutting. If one is smaller, stretch it gently on the hand while still damp and warm. Differences of up to 1 cm usually disappear after wearing; larger differences are not recoverable.

items.problem: The wrist edge is ragged or pilling after cutting
items.cause: The fabric at the wrist was cut before it was fully felted, or the scissors compressed the still-damp edge
items.fix: Ensure the felt is firm throughout before cutting the wrist edge. Use sharp scissors and cut in one clean motion. If the raw edge pills or loosens, wet it with warm soapy water and rub it between your palms for 30 seconds to consolidate the cut fibres.

content.text: Finishing and care

Rinse both mittens thoroughly in lukewarm water until the water runs clear. Press out excess water by rolling each mitten in a clean towel and pressing firmly; do not wring. Pull to shape over a hand form or a rolled towel stuffed into the mitten shape, and leave to dry at room temperature. The felt stiffens as it dries. Hand-wash when needed in cool water with a small amount of wool wash; do not machine-wash and do not tumble-dry. Dry flat or on a mitten form after each wash.
